body.text: A function is a named, reusable block of code. You define it once and call it by name whenever you need it. Without functions, you copy and paste the same logic everywhere — and when you need to change it, you change it in every place.

body.desc: Define a block of code named 'greet' — does not run yet
Run that block of code right now

lines: console.log('This runs every time showMessage is called.');
// Writing showMessage without () does NOT call it

body.label: Defining vs calling
body.text: Writing function showMessage() {} only creates the function — it does not run it. Writing showMessage() runs it. The parentheses are what trigger execution.

exercises.title: Define and call
tasks: Write a function called sayHello that logs 'Hello!' to the console.
Below the function definition, call it three times.
Write a second function called sayGoodbye that logs 'Goodbye!'.
Call both functions in alternating order three times each.
Predict: if you write sayHello without the (), does it run? Try it and read the console output.
exercises.hint: A function definition starts with the word 'function'. Calling it uses the function name followed by ().

checks.label: sayHello() exists and can be called
sayGoodbye() exists and can be called

exercises.title: Functions with parameters
tasks: Write a function called double that takes a number and logs that number multiplied by 2.
Call it with five different numbers.
Write a function called describe that takes a name and a color, logs: '[name] likes [color].'
Challenge: write a function called add that takes two numbers and returns (not logs) their sum. Store the result and log it.
exercises.hint: A parameter is declared inside the parentheses: function double(num) { }. Use num inside the function body.
